skill.name: containerizing-applications
skill.description: Create production-ready Dockerfiles, Docker Compose files, and Helm chart patterns for common web applications. The skill emphasizes multi-stage builds, service networking, health checks, vulnerability scanning, and safer secret handling.
skill.summary: Build Docker, Compose, and Helm configurations with production security guidance.
skill.author: Asmayaseen

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported 114 potential issues, but review found the cited patterns are documentation examples, Dockerfile snippets, and a local verification script. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or hidden network execution was found. Publish with a warning because the skill teaches Docker, Helm, Trivy, and build commands that can change local or remote infrastructure if users run them.

medium_findings.title: Operational Container Commands Require User Review
medium_findings.description: The skill includes Helm install and upgrade examples plus Docker buildx push examples. These are legitimate for a containerization skill, but running them can deploy workloads or push images to configured registries.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly documented examples, not hidden execution. Risk depends on whether a user or agent runs them against real infrastructure.

medium_findings.title: Secret Handling Examples Need Careful Use
medium_findings.description: The static credential findings point to BuildKit secret mounts and .dockerignore entries that are intended to prevent secret leakage. They are not exfiltration, but users must avoid replacing the examples with insecure secret copying into image layers.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly describe mounting secrets without storing them and excluding sensitive files. The remaining concern is safe adaptation by downstream users.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: Most external command detections are Dockerfile, YAML, Bash, or Helm examples inside documentation. They do not execute during skill loading, and no dynamic command construction was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious patterns appear inside fenced examples and prose. There is no evidence that the skill automatically invokes these commands.

low_findings.title: Verification Script Performs Local Structure Checks Only
low_findings.description: The verification script reads SKILL.md, checks for required section names, prints status, and exits. The subprocess import is unused, and no shell command execution or network activity is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The full script is visible and limited to local Path checks and string matching. No evidence found of reconnaissance beyond checking the skill file content.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs And IPs Are Local Container Examples
low_findings.description: Detected URLs and IP addresses are localhost, Docker service names, health checks, and allowed-host examples. No external callback endpoint or data exfiltration destination was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network strings are tied to container networking examples and health checks. No evidence found of unauthorized outbound network behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Infrastructure-Changing Commands In Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents commands for Helm installs, image scans, Docker SBOM inspection, and multi-architecture image pushes. These are expected capabilities but should be reviewed before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are present and can affect infrastructure if run. The semantic context supports legitimate DevOps use rather than malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive File Names In Safety Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill references .env files, key files, credentials.json, and .netrc in examples about excluding or mounting secrets safely. This is a scanner trigger but the context is defensive guidance.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sensitive paths appear in BuildKit and .dockerignore safety patterns. No evidence found that the skill reads, copies, or transmits those files.

content.value_statement: Teams often lose time converting local applications into reliable containers and Kubernetes packages. This skill provides Docker, Compose, Helm, scanning, and secret-handling patterns for production-ready deployments.
actual_capabilities: Drafts multi-stage Dockerfile patterns for Python, Node.js, Next.js, and similar web services.
Creates Docker Compose service layouts with health checks, ports, environment variables, and service networking.
Outlines Helm chart structure, values patterns, and common install or upgrade commands.
Explains container networking pitfalls such as localhost, service names, CORS origins, and health check hosts.
Recommends production security practices including minimal images, Trivy scans, SBOMs, and BuildKit secret mounts.
Includes a local verification script that checks required skill documentation sections.
limitations: It provides patterns and guidance, not a full deployment platform or registry integration.
Generated commands can affect real Docker, Helm, or Kubernetes environments if executed without review.
Application-specific dependencies, ports, and readiness checks still need project validation.
It does not prove that a final image is vulnerability-free or compliant with every organization policy.

use_cases.title: Package A Web App For Containers
use_cases.description: Create a Dockerfile and Compose layout for a local application that needs repeatable development and deployment behavior.

use_cases.title: Prepare Kubernetes Release Assets
use_cases.description: Turn containerized services into a basic Helm chart with values, services, deployment templates, and configurable image tags.

use_cases.title: Improve Container Security Reviews
use_cases.description: Apply practical checks for minimal images, non-root execution, vulnerability scans, SBOMs, and secret handling before release.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Basic Dockerfile
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a production-ready Dockerfile for my application. Ask for the language, start command, port, dependency files, and build steps before drafting it.

prompt_templates.title: Add Docker Compose Services
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Docker Compose setup for my web app, API, and database. Include health checks, service names, environment variables, and startup ordering.

prompt_templates.title: Package With Helm
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Helm chart plan for my containerized application. Include values, deployment settings, service ports, ingress options, secrets, and upgrade commands.

prompt_templates.title: Harden A Container Build
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my container build for production security. Check base images, user permissions, secret handling, SBOM generation, vulnerability scanning, and multi-architecture build needs.

output_examples.input: A FastAPI service needs containerization for production.
output: A multi-stage Python image plan with a dedicated runtime user.
A Compose service definition with an API port, database URL, and health check.
Security notes for base image selection, vulnerability scanning, and secret handling.

output_examples.input: A Next.js frontend must connect to an API in Docker Compose.
output: A clear split between browser-facing public URLs and server-side container service URLs.
A Compose networking plan that avoids localhost mistakes inside containers.
Build argument and runtime environment guidance for predictable deployments.

output_examples.input: A team wants to ship a containerized service to Kubernetes.
output: A Helm chart outline with values, deployment, service, config, secret, and ingress areas.
Release guidance for image tags, resource limits, and upgrade workflows.
A checklist for scans, SBOMs, non-root execution, and sensitive file exclusion.

best_practices: Review generated Docker, Helm, and Kubernetes commands before running them against real environments.
Use BuildKit secret mounts and .dockerignore rules instead of copying credentials into image layers.
Validate health checks, service names, resource limits, and CORS origins with the actual application.
anti_patterns: Do not bake private tokens, .env files, certificates, or credentials into container images.
Do not use localhost for server-to-server communication inside Docker networks unless it is truly local to one container.
Do not push images or run Helm upgrades from generated examples without checking registry, namespace, and cluster context.

faq.question: Does this skill run Docker or Helm automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides commands and configuration patterns. Users or agents must choose whether to execute them.

faq.question: Can it create Docker Compose files for multiple services?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes patterns for web, API, database, health checks, environment variables, ports, and service dependencies.

faq.question: Does it support Kubernetes packaging?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes Helm chart structure, values files, templates, install commands, and upgrade commands.

faq.question: How does it handle secrets?
faq.answer: It recommends BuildKit secret mounts and .dockerignore exclusions so secrets are not stored in image layers.

faq.question: Can it guarantee secure container images?
faq.answer: No. It recommends safer patterns and scans, but final security depends on dependencies, base images, and deployment policies.

faq.question: Is it useful with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is structured for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows that generate or review container assets.
